2477	  check the value on the generators \(r' \otimes m\). The left side sends
2478	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto r' \cdot \rho(m) = r' \cdot ((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m) =
2479	  (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\), using that \(\varepsilon^{\mathrm{alg}}\) reads the extension of
2480	  scalars by acting through the \(R'\)-scalar. The inverse regrouping isomorphism of
2481	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv} sends, by definition,
2482	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\) (in the \(A \otimes_R R'\) orientation). The two
2483	  values agree on every generator, so the maps are equal. No flatness hypothesis is used.
2484	\end{proof}

1954	[The kernel pair's lower module is stable under the full family]
1955	  \label{lem:graded_ker_stable_full}
1956	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_stable_full}
1957	  \uses{lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute}
1958	  Let \((N, N')\) be a length-\((r+1)\) subquotient datum
1959	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) and
1960	  \(x = t_r\). Then the kernel pair's lower module \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is stable
1961	  under \emph{every} \(t_i\) (\(0 \le i \le r\)), not only the reduced family: each
1962	  \(t_i\) preserves \(N\) and preserves the preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) because it
1963	  commutes with \(x\) and preserves \(N'\)
1964	  (\cref{lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute}). This full stability is what allows the
1965	  kernel subquotient's finiteness to be measured over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)
1966	  before the transfer drops the last variable.
1967	\end{lemma}

1973	  \label{lem:graded_coker_stable_full}

1975	  \uses{lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute}

1977	  stable under every \(t_i\) (\(0 \le i \le r\)): each \(t_i\) preserves \(N'\) and

1979	  \(N\) (\cref{lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute}), and a map preserves a sum of

1987	[Kernel constructor of a subquotient datum]
1988	  \label{def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker}

1990	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,

1993	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer,
1994	    lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_numerator}
1995	  From a length-\((r+1)\) subquotient datum \((N, N')\) with endomorphisms
1996	  \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) and \(x = t_r\) (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}), the
1997	  \emph{kernel constructor} produces the length-\(r\) datum
1998	  \[
1999	    \ker D \;=\; \bigl(\,N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\ N'\,\bigr)
2000	  \]
2001	  on the reduced family \((t_0, \dots, t_{r-1})\). Its
2002	  non-finiteness fields are supplied by the ambient kernel calculus: homogeneity of
2003	  \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous}), the nesting
2004	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_ker_le}), and stability under the
2005	  reduced family (\cref{lem:graded_ker_stable_full}). The finiteness witness
2006	  is obtained by first shrinking the numerator from \(N\) to

2008	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-finiteness of the kernel subquotient, then transferring
2009	  down one variable (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}), valid because
2010	  \(x\) annihilates the kernel subquotient (\cref{lem:graded_ker_annihilate}). This
2011	  realises the kernel half of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} as a genuine
2012	  length-\(r\) datum.

2044	\subsubsection*{The degreewise difference identity}
2045	
2046	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2047	[$D_5$ -- degreewise rank--nullity difference]
2048	  \label{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}
2049	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.degreewise_finrank_diff}
2050	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
2051	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
2052	  Let \(\varphi : V \to W\) be a \(\kappa\)-linear map between finite-dimensional
2053	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. Then
2054	  \[
2055	    \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V
2056	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi).
2057	  \]
2058	  Applied degreewise with \(\varphi = (x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\),
2059	  \(V = \mathcal{M}_n\), \(W = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) -- so that \(\ker\varphi\) is the
2060	  degree-\(n\) piece \(K_n\) of the kernel subquotient and
2061	  \(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x \mathcal{M}_n\) the
2062	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(C_{n+1}\) of the cokernel subquotient
2063	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, via the split \(G_1\)
2064	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq} and the degree shift
2065	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) -- this gives, for every \(n\),
2066	  \[
2067	    \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_n
2068	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa C_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa K_n,
2069	  \]
2070	  which is the difference identity \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) consumed by
2071	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}. No graded structure is used in proving the
2072	  underlying vector-space identity -- it is pure rank--nullity.
2073	\end{lemma}
2074	
2075	\begin{proof}
2076	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
2077	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
2078	    \leanok
2079	  By rank--nullity (\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}),
2080	  \(\dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}
2081	  \varphi)\), so \(\dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa V -
2082	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). By additivity on the short exact sequence
2083	  \(0 \to \operatorname{range}\varphi \to W \to W/\operatorname{range}\varphi \to 0\)
2084	  (\cref{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}),
2085	  \(\dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W -
2086	  \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V +
2087	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). Rearranging gives
2088	  \(\dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) -
2089	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). The degreewise application identifies
2090	  \(\ker(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\) and
2091	  \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}/\operatorname{range}(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\)
2092	  with the degree-\(n\) piece of the kernel subquotient and the degree-\((n+1)\)
2093	  piece of the cokernel subquotient (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
2094	  the latter because the degree-\((n+1)\) graded piece of the homogeneous submodule
2095	  \(x\mathcal{M}\) is exactly \(x \mathcal{M}_n\)
2096	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}).
2097	\end{proof}

2099	\subsubsection*{The base case ($r = 0$)}
2100	
2101	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2102	[Base-case finiteness over the empty polynomial ring]
2103	  \label{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2104	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2105	  Let \(Q\) be a \(\kappa\)-vector space that is also a module over
2106	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa)\) for an \emph{empty} index set
2107	  \(\sigma\) -- in particular \(\sigma = \{0, \dots, r-1\}\) with \(r = 0\) -- in a way
2108	  compatible with the \(\kappa\)-action (scalar tower). If \(Q\) is module-finite over
2109	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa)\), then \(Q\) is finite-dimensional over
2110	  \(\kappa\). Indeed for empty \(\sigma\) the polynomial ring is
2111	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa) \cong \kappa\), a finite \(\kappa\)-module,
2112	  so finiteness of \(Q\) over it transfers to finiteness over \(\kappa\) along the scalar
2113	  tower. This is the length-zero input to the ambient subquotient induction
2114	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}): when \(r = 0\) the subquotient \(N/N'\) is a
2115	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space, hence \(\mathrm{hilb}\) is eventually zero.
2116	\end{lemma}
2117	
2118	\begin{lemma}
2119	
2120	\leanok
2121	[independence of images modulo the kernel]
2122	  \label{lem:graded_iSupIndep_map_of_mem_ker_sup}
2123	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.iSupIndep_map_of_mem_ker_sup}
2124	  Let \(\pi : A \to Q\) be a \(\kappa\)-linear map and \((g_i)_{i \in \iota}\) a family of
2125	  subspaces of \(A\) that is \emph{independent modulo \(\ker\pi\)}: for each \(i\), every
2126	  \(a \in g_i\) that also lies in \(\ker\pi + \sum_{j \ne i} g_j\) already lies in
2127	  \(\ker\pi\). Then the image family \((\pi(g_i))_{i \in \iota}\) is independent
2128	  (\(\mathrm{iSupIndep}\)) in \(Q\). Ring-agnostic lattice core of the base-case
2129	  independence step below; applied to the quotient map \(N \twoheadrightarrow N/N'\).
2130	\end{lemma}
2131	\begin{proof}
2132	
2133	\leanok
2134	  By the disjointness criterion for independence it suffices to show, for each \(i\),
2135	  that \(\pi(g_i) \cap \sum_{j \ne i} \pi(g_j) = 0\). Take \(q\) in that intersection;
2136	  write \(q = \pi(a)\) with \(a \in g_i\), and (pushing the sum through \(\pi\), since
2137	  \(\pi(\sum_{j\ne i} g_j) = \sum_{j\ne i}\pi(g_j)\)) \(q = \pi(b)\) with
2138	  \(b \in \sum_{j \ne i} g_j\). Then \(a - b \in \ker\pi\), so
2139	  \(a = (a-b) + b \in \ker\pi + \sum_{j \ne i} g_j\). By the hypothesis applied at \(i\),
2140	  \(a \in \ker\pi\), whence \(q = \pi(a) = 0\).
2141	\end{proof}
2142	
2143	\begin{lemma}
2144	
2145	\leanok
2146	[Base case: the length-zero subquotient Hilbert function vanishes eventually]
2147	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero}
2148	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_base_eventuallyZero}
2149	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb,
2150	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite,
2151	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep, lem:graded_iSupIndep_map_of_mem_ker_sup,
2152	    lem:finite_ne_bot_of_iSupIndep_mathlib, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
2153	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
2154	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
2155	  % 00K1), proof, base case (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex,
2156	  % L13908-L13910).
2157	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2158	  % "We prove this by induction on the minimal number of
2159	  %  generators of $S_1$. If this number is $0$, then
2160	  %  $M_n = 0$ for all $n \gg 0$ and the result holds."
2161	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (base case of the induction).}
2162	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(0\)
2163	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}): a homogeneous pair \(N' \le N\) of the fixed
2164	  ambient graded module \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) with \emph{no} endomorphisms,
2165	  whose finiteness witness says the subquotient \(Q_0 := N/N'\) is module-finite over
2166	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\varnothing, \kappa)\). Then the ambient Hilbert
2167	  function vanishes eventually: there is \(K \in \mathbb{N}\) with
2168	  \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = 0\) for all \(n > K\). Consequently
2169	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\) (\cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}),
2170	  which is the base case of the ambient subquotient induction
2171	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}).
2172	\end{lemma}
2173	
2174	\begin{proof}
2175	  \uses{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite,
2176	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
2177	    lem:finite_ne_bot_of_iSupIndep_mathlib, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
2178	    \leanok
2179	  Since the index set of variables is empty,
2180	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\varnothing, \kappa) \cong \kappa\), so the finiteness
2181	  witness makes the subquotient \(Q_0 = N/N'\) a \emph{finite-dimensional}
2182	  \(\kappa\)-vector space
2183	  (\cref{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}). For each
2184	  degree \(n\), composing the inclusion of the homogeneous piece
2185	  \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n \hookrightarrow N\) with the quotient projection
2186	  \(N \twoheadrightarrow Q_0\) gives a \(\kappa\)-linear map
2187	  \(\psi_n : N \cap \mathcal{M}_n \to Q_0\), \(m \mapsto [m]\), whose image is the
2188	  class of the degree-\(n\) homogeneous piece inside the subquotient.
2189	
2190	  \emph{Step 1: independence of the images.} The family
2191	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n)\bigr)_{n}\) is \(\mathrm{iSupIndep}\) in
2192	  \(Q_0\). The reason is the ambient direct-sum grading of \(M\): the homogeneous
2193	  pieces \(\mathcal{M}_n\) form an independent family
2194	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}). An element of
2195	  \(\bigsqcup_{j \ne n} \operatorname{range}(\psi_j)\) is a finite sum of classes of
2196	  homogeneous elements of degrees \(j \ne n\); since \(N'\) is homogeneous, the
2197	  degree-\(n\) homogeneous component of any representative of such a class lies in
2198	  \(N'\), so the class has zero degree-\(n\) component in \(Q_0\). A nonzero class in
2199	  \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n)\), by contrast, is represented by a homogeneous
2200	  element of degree \(n\) not in \(N'\), so has nonzero degree-\(n\) component.
2201	  Comparing degree-\(n\) components forces the intersection
2202	  \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n) \cap \bigsqcup_{j \ne n} \operatorname{range}(\psi_j)
2203	  = 0\); independence follows.
2204	  % NOTE: ROUTE (b). Prove the iSupIndep by direct membership analysis in
2205	  %   ⨆_{j ≠ n} range(ψ j), reading off the degree-n homogeneous component
2206	  %   directly inside Q₀'s fixed κ-module structure.
2207	  %   No outgoing linear map out of Q₀ is constructed, so no scalar ring other than κ
2208	  %   is ever introduced.
2209	  % NOTE: ROUTE (a). Building a κ-linear detector
2210	  %   Φ : Q₀ → M/N', [m] ↦ [π_n m] (degree-n projection) via descent through the
2211	  %   subquotient fails: the descended map carries the scalar action of Q₀'s base
2212	  %   ring, but the target M/N' carries only a κ-module structure -- a scalar-ring
2213	  %   incompatibility. Route (b) avoids this by working entirely within Q₀.
2214	
2215	  \emph{Step 2: finiteness of the support.} Because \(Q_0\) is finite-dimensional and
2216	  the ranges \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n)\) are independent, only finitely many of
2217	  them are nonzero: the set \(\{\,n : \operatorname{range}(\psi_n) \ne 0\,\}\) is
2218	  finite (\cref{lem:finite_ne_bot_of_iSupIndep_mathlib}). In particular it is bounded
2219	  above, by some \(K \in \mathbb{N}\).
2220	
2221	  \emph{Step 3: eventual vanishing.} For \(n > K\) we have
2222	  \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n) = 0\), i.e.\ every homogeneous element of
2223	  \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\) has class \(0\) in \(Q_0 = N/N'\); equivalently
2224	  \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n \le N'\), hence \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n = N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n\)
2225	  and
2226	  \[
2227	    \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; \dim_\kappa(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
2228	      - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n) \;=\; 0 .
2229	  \]
2230	  Thus \(\mathrm{hilb}\) is eventually zero, and
2231	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero} gives \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\).
2232	\end{proof}

2234	\subsubsection*{The ambient subquotient induction}

2236	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2237	[Rationality of the ambient subquotient Hilbert function]
2238	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}
2239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_hilbertSeries_rational}
2240	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
2241	    def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker, def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker,
2242	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
2243	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
2244	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump,
2245	    lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero,
2246	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2247	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
2248	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
2249	  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
2250	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (induction on the number of
2251	  degree-one generators).}
2252	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(r\)
2253	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with ambient Hilbert function
2254	  \(\mathrm{hilb}\). Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\)
2255	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}): the difference \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
2256	  - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), the coefficient
2257	  sequence of a rational series \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-r}\) with \(p \in
2258	  \mathbb{Q}[X]\). The induction is on the length \(r\), and every object that
2259	  appears is an ambient pair of homogeneous submodules of the fixed graded module
2260	  \(M\).
2261	\end{lemma}
2262	
2263	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, induction set-up (read
2264	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
2265	% "We prove this by induction on the minimal number of
2266	%  generators of $S_1$. If this number is $0$, then
2267	%  $M_n = 0$ for all $n \gg 0$ and the result holds.
2268	%  To prove the induction step, let $x\in S_1$
2269	%  be one of a minimal set of generators, such that
2270	%  the induction hypothesis applies to the
2271	%  graded ring $S/(x)$."
2272	\begin{proof}
2273	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
2274	    def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker, def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker,
2275	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
2276	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
2277	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump,
2278	    lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero,
2279	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2280	    \leanok
2281	  We induct on the length \(r\).
2282	
2283	  \emph{Base case \(r = 0\).} A length-zero datum has a finiteness witness making the
2284	  subquotient \(N/N'\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space
2285	  (\cref{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}); its degreewise
2286	  images form an independent family inside that finite-dimensional space, so only
2287	  finitely many are nonzero and \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = 0\) for \(n \gg 0\)
2288	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero}). By
2289	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero} an eventually-zero function is rational of order
2290	  \(0\), giving \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\).
2291	
2292	  \emph{Inductive step \(r \ge 1\).} Set \(x = t_{r-1}\). By the kernel/cokernel
2293	  closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}) the kernel subquotient
2294	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and the cokernel subquotient
2295	  \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) are subquotient data of length \(r-1\), assembled by
2296	  the kernel and cokernel constructors (\cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker},
2297	  \cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker}) whose finiteness witnesses come from the
2298	  abstract finiteness transfer (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}). The
2299	  induction hypothesis applied
2300	  to \(K\) and \(C\) gives \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and
2301	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}). By the degreewise
2302	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
2303	  \[
2304	    \mathrm{hilb}(n+1) - \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)
2305	    \qquad \text{for all } n .
2306	  \]
2307	  Feeding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\), \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and this
2308	  difference identity into the inductive-step engine
2309	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}) yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\). (The
2310	  common order \(r-1\) for \(h_C\) and \(h_K\) is arranged, if necessary, by raising
2311	  the lower one with \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump}.) This completes the induction.
2312	\end{proof}
2313	
2314	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
2315	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
2316	
2317	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
2318	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
2319	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
2320	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
2321	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.
2322	
2323	\begin{definition}
2324	
2325	\leanok
2326	[Annihilator ideal sheaf of a sheaf of modules]
2327	  \label{def:modules_annihilator}
2328	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator}
2329	  \uses{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
2330	  % NOTE: The definition itself is formalized and axiom-clean; the inclusion direction
2331	  % \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U) \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\)
2332	  % (\cref{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}) is closed. The reverse inclusion, which identifies
2333	  % the annihilator sheaf with the module-annihilator on affine opens, remains bridge-gated on
2334	  % \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}.
2335	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
2336	  % Polynomials" (read from
2337	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
2338	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2339	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
2340	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
2341	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
2342	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
2343	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
2344	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of
2345	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type}
2346	  (over a locally noetherian base, so that on each affine open \(U\) the section
2347	  module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is a finitely generated \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module).
2348	  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
2349	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) defined on affine opens: for each affine open
2350	  \(U \subseteq X\),
2351	  \[
2352	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
2353	    \;=\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr)
2354	    \;\subseteq\; \mathcal{O}_X(U),
2355	  \]
2356	  the annihilator of the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\). These
2357	  local data patch to a coherent ideal sheaf on \(X\) whose vanishing locus is
2358	  the set-theoretic support of \(\mathcal{F}\).
2359	
2360	  \emph{Well-definedness on basic opens.} The coherence condition for an ideal
2361	  sheaf requires that, for an affine open \(U\) and \(f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
2362	  the section \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)\) restricts correctly to the basic
2363	  open \(D(f) \subseteq U\), i.e. that
2364	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(D(f))}(\mathcal{F}(D(f)))\) is the extension of
2365	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\) along the localization map
2366	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \to \mathcal{O}_X(D(f))\). This is exactly where the two
2367	  hypotheses enter. The bridge
2368	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} supplies, for a quasi-coherent
2369	  finite-type \(\mathcal{F}\), that \(\mathcal{O}_X(D(f)) = (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\)
2370	  and that the restriction \(\mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{F}(D(f))\) exhibits
2371	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(f))\) as the localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
2372	  \mathcal{F}(U)\); the algebra engine
2373	  \cref{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map} then yields, using that
2374	  \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is finitely generated, the required identity
2375	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{(\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f}\bigl((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
2376	  \mathcal{F}(U)\bigr) = \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U)) \cdot
2377	  (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\). Thus the local annihilators are compatible with basic-open
2378	  restriction, which is the patching datum that makes \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\)
2379	  a well-defined ideal sheaf. The finite-type hypothesis is genuinely required:
2380	  without finite generation of \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) the annihilator need not commute
2381	  with localization, and the local data fail to patch.
2382	
2383	  \emph{Note.} Mathlib does not carry an annihilator for sheaves of modules on a scheme.
2384	\end{definition}
2385	
2386	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2387	[Annihilator ideal bounded by the module annihilator on affine opens]
2388	  \label{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}
2389	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator_ideal_le}
2390	  \uses{def:modules_annihilator}

2392	  \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open. Then the section over \(U\) of the annihilator
2393	  ideal sheaf \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}) is
2394	  contained in the module-annihilator of the sections of \(\mathcal{F}\) over
2395	  \(U\):
2396	  \[
2397	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
2398	    \;\subseteq\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr).
2399	  \]
2400	  This is the inclusion direction available directly from the definition of
2401	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\); the reverse inclusion is the basic-open coherence
2402	  statement gated on \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}. Proved directly
2403	  in Lean. It supports the well-definedness of the schematic support
2404	  (\cref{def:schematic_support}).
2405	\end{lemma}
2406	
2407	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2408	[Annihilator of a finitely generated module commutes with
2409	  localization]
2410	  \label{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map}
2411	  \lean{Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map}
2412	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(S \subseteq R\) be a multiplicative
2413	  submonoid, and let \(R_S = S^{-1}R\) be the localization of \(R\) at \(S\). Let
2414	  \(M\) be a \emph{finitely generated} \(R\)-module and let \(f : M \to M_S\) be
2415	  an \(R\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_S\) as the localized module \(S^{-1}M\)
2416	  (i.e.\ \(f\) is a localization of \(M\) at \(S\)). Then the annihilator of
2417	  \(M_S\) over \(R_S\) is the extension of the annihilator of \(M\) along the
2418	  structure map \(R \to R_S\):
2419	  \[
2420	    \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)
2421	    \;=\; (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S
2422	    \;=\; \mathrm{map}\bigl(R \to R_S\bigr)\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}_R M\bigr).
2423	  \]
2424	  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
2425	  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).
2426	
2427	  \emph{The finite-generation hypothesis is essential.} For a module that is not
2428	  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
2429	  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
2430	  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
2431	  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
2432	  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.
2433	\end{lemma}
2434	
2435	\begin{proof}
2436	  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
2437	  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
2438	  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
2439	  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
2440	  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).
2441	
2442	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
2443	  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
2444	  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
2445	  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
2446	  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
2447	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
2448	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
2449	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
2450	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
2451	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
2452	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
2453	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
2454	\end{proof}

2491	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2492	  % "Let $R$ be a ring. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. Let $\widetilde M$

2495	  %  [...]

2498	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", \S 7, Lemma on the sheaves
2499	  \(\widetilde M\) on \(\Spec(R)\), item (4).}
2500	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a \emph{quasi-coherent} sheaf
2501	  of modules on \(X\). Let \(U \subseteq X\) be an
2502	  affine open and \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then:
2503	  \begin{enumerate}

2505	      of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\)
2506	      (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); and
2507	    \item the section-restriction map
2508	      \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}\bigl(D(f)\bigr)\) exhibits
2509	      \(\mathcal{M}(D(f))\) as the localized module
2510	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\mathcal{M}(U)\); that is, it is a localization
2511	      of the \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(\mathcal{M}(U)\) at the submonoid
2512	      \(\mathrm{powers}\,f = \{1, f, f^2, \dots\}\).

2516	\begin{proof}
2517	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2518	  Part (1) is \cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}, the Mathlib statement
2519	  that on an affine open \(U\) the sections over the basic open \(D(f)\) form the
2520	  localization of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\).
2521	
2522	  For part (2), identify the affine open \(U\) with \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\). Over
2523	  this affine, a quasi-coherent sheaf is \(\widetilde{N}\) for the
2524	  \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\), the sheaf associated to
2525	  \(N\) (no finiteness of \(N\) is required for this step). The Spec-local
2526	  computation of the sections of \(\widetilde{N}\) on a
2527	  basic open gives \(\Gamma\bigl(D(f), \widetilde{N}\bigr) = N_f\) as an
2528	  \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)-module, and the restriction map
2529	  \(\Gamma(\Spec \Gamma(X,U), \widetilde{N}) = N \to N_f\) is precisely the
2530	  localization homomorphism \(N \to (\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1} N\). Transporting
2531	  this identification across the affine isomorphism
2532	  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, U)\) -- under which \(\mathcal{M}|_U\) corresponds to
2533	  \(\widetilde{N}\) by quasi-coherence and the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq U\)

2535	  that the restriction \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}(D(f))\) is the
2536	  localization of \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\), i.e.\ it is a
2537	  localized module at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\). The substantive content is this
2538	  transport of the Spec-local localization fact for the sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\)
2539	  to a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{M}\) via the affine identification.
2540	\end{proof}

2612	\begin{lemma}[The three-field localized-module predicate]
2613	  \label{lem:isLocalizedModule_constructor_mathlib}

2616	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Module.LocalizedModule.Basic}).}
2617	  For a submonoid \(S \subseteq R\) and an \(R\)-linear map \(g : M \to M'\), the predicate
2618	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}\,S\,g\) is the conjunction of three pointwise statements:
2619	  \emph{(map\_units)} each \(s \in S\) acts invertibly on \(M'\);
2620	  \emph{(surj)} every element of \(M'\) equals \(s^{-1}\,g(m)\) for some \(s \in S\), \(m \in M\); and
2621	  \emph{(exists\_of\_eq)} if \(g(m_1) = g(m_2)\) then \(s\,m_1 = s\,m_2\) for some \(s \in S\).
2622	  Supplying these three fields constructs \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}\,S\,g\).

1258	  \label{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}

1260	  Let \(f : X \to Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes. Then the pullback functor on
1261	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is
1262	  one half of an equivalence of categories
1263	  \(Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \simeq X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\), with quasi-inverse
1264	  \((f^{-1})^*\).
1265	\end{lemma}
1266	\begin{proof}
1267	
1268	  Assemble the equivalence from the functor \(f^*\) and the candidate inverse
1269	  \((f^{-1})^*\). The unit and counit isomorphisms are built from the pseudofunctor
1270	  coherences of pullback: \((f^{-1})^* \circ f^* \cong (f \circ f^{-1})^* = \mathrm{id}^*
1271	  \cong \mathrm{id}\) and symmetrically, using
1272	  \(f \circ f^{-1} = \mathrm{id}_Y\), \(f^{-1} \circ f = \mathrm{id}_X\)
1273	  (composition-of-pullbacks, congruence under equal maps, and pullback-of-identity).
1274	  The triangle identities are forced by these coherences.
1275	\end{proof}

1466	\begin{lemma}[Unit-across-conjugate coherence]
1467	  \label{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib}
1468	  \lean{CategoryTheory.unit_conjugateEquiv}
1469	  \mathlibok
1470	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1471	  Let \(\mathrm{adj}_1 : L_1 \dashv R_1\) and \(\mathrm{adj}_2 : L_2 \dashv R_2\) be two
1472	  adjunctions between the same pair of categories, with units \(\eta_1\) and \(\eta_2\), and let
1473	  \(\alpha : L_2 \Rightarrow L_1\) be a natural transformation of the left adjoints. Write
1474	  \(\operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_1, \mathrm{adj}_2)\) for the conjugation (mate) bijection,
1475	  which carries \(\alpha\) to a natural transformation \(R_1 \Rightarrow R_2\) of the right
1476	  adjoints. Then for every object \(c\),

2854	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule.linearEquiv}\,S\,g\,g'\)
2855	  (\cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_linearEquiv_mathlib}): both compose with \(g\) to give \(g'\), so they
2856	  coincide by uniqueness of maps out of a localization
2857	  (\cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_linearMap_ext_mathlib}). A linear equivalence is bijective, hence so
2858	  is \(h\).
2859	\end{proof}

2861	\begin{lemma}[Basis-wise isomorphism of sheaves on $\Spec R$]
2862	  \label{lem:isIso_sheaf_of_isIso_app_basicOpen}
2863	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_sheaf_of_isIso_app_basicOpen}
2864	  \uses{lem:isBasis_basic_opens_mathlib, lem:stalkFunctor_map_injective_of_isBasis_mathlib,
2865	    lem:isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso_mathlib}
2866	  \textit{Upgrades a per-basic-open isomorphism to an isomorphism of sheaves.}
2867	  Let \(\alpha : F \to G\) be a morphism of sheaves of \(R\)-modules on \(\Spec R\) whose component
2868	  on every basic open \(D(f)\) is an isomorphism. Then \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism.
2869	\end{lemma}
2870	\begin{proof}
2871	  \uses{lem:isBasis_basic_opens_mathlib, lem:stalkFunctor_map_injective_of_isBasis_mathlib,
2872	    lem:isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso_mathlib}
2873	  The basic opens form a basis of \(\Spec R\) (\cref{lem:isBasis_basic_opens_mathlib}). Bijectivity
2874	  of every component \(\alpha_{D(f)}\) gives, in particular, injectivity on each basis open, hence
2875	  injectivity on every stalk (\cref{lem:stalkFunctor_map_injective_of_isBasis_mathlib});
2876	  surjectivity on stalks follows by lifting germs through the bijective basic-open components. A
2877	  morphism that is an isomorphism on every stalk is an isomorphism
2878	  (\cref{lem:isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso_mathlib}).
2879	\end{proof}

2881	\begin{lemma}[$\mathrm{IsIso}\,M.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}$ from per-basic-open section localization
2882	(G1-assemble)]
2883	  \label{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
2884	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
2885	  \uses{lem:isIso_sheaf_of_isIso_app_basicOpen, lem:bijective_comp_of_localizations,
2886	    lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}
2887	  \textit{The G1-assemble reduction: section-localization on every basic open forces the
2888	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\)-counit to be an isomorphism.}
2889	  Let \(M\) be a sheaf of modules on \(\Spec R\), and suppose that for every \(f \in R\) the section
2890	  restriction \(\Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\) exhibits the target as
2891	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) over \(R\) -- exactly the conclusion of G1-core
2892	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization}). Then the counit \(M.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) is an
2893	  isomorphism.
2894	\end{lemma}
2895	\begin{proof}
2896	  \uses{lem:isIso_sheaf_of_isIso_app_basicOpen, lem:bijective_comp_of_localizations,
2897	    lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}
2898	  Write \(N = \Gamma(M, \top)\). On each basic open \(D(f)\), the component of
2899	  \(\mathrm{modulesSpecToSheaf}(M.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ})\) is a map between two localizations of \(N\)
2900	  at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\): the source \(\Gamma(\widetilde N, D(f))\) localizes \(N\) via the affine
2901	  tilde map (\cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}) and the target \(\Gamma(M, D(f))\)
2902	  localizes \(N\) by hypothesis, the two being intertwined by the compatibility
2903	  \(\mathrm{toOpen\_fromTildeΓ\_app}\). Hence the component is bijective
2904	  (\cref{lem:bijective_comp_of_localizations}), so an isomorphism. By
2905	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheaf_of_isIso_app_basicOpen} the sheaf morphism
2906	  \(\mathrm{modulesSpecToSheaf}(M.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ})\) is then an isomorphism, and since
2907	  \(\mathrm{modulesSpecToSheaf}\) is fully faithful it reflects isomorphisms, giving
2908	  \(\mathrm{IsIso}\,M.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\).
2909	\end{proof}

2911	\begin{lemma}[$\mathrm{IsIso}\,M.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}$ characterized by section localization]
2912	  \label{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
2913	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
2914	  \uses{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict,
2915	    lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ}
2916	  \textit{The two engines packaged as an equivalence.}
2917	  For a sheaf of modules \(M\) on \(\Spec R\), the counit \(M.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) is an
2918	  isomorphism if and only if for every \(f \in R\) the section restriction
2919	  \(\Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\) exhibits the target as
2920	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) over \(R\).
2921	\end{lemma}
2922	\begin{proof}
2923	  \uses{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict,
2924	    lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ}
2925	  The forward direction is \cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ} (the affine
2926	  engine, applied for each \(f\)); the reverse direction is
2927	  \cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}.
2928	\end{proof}

2931	
2932	\leanok
2933	[Schematic support of a sheaf of modules]
2934	  \label{def:schematic_support}
2935	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.schematicSupport}
2936	  \uses{def:modules_annihilator, lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le,
2937	    def:schematic_support_immersion}
2938	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
2939	  % Polynomials" (read from
2940	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
2941	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2942	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
2943	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
2944	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
2945	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
2946	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
2947	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\). The
2948	  \emph{schematic support} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the closed subscheme of \(X\)
2949	  cut out by its annihilator ideal sheaf (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}):
2950	  \[
2951	    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F})
2952	    \;=\; V\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\bigr) \;\hookrightarrow\; X,
2953	  \]
2954	  equipped with its canonical closed immersion into \(X\). This realises
2955	  Nitsure's ``schematic support of \(\mathcal{F}\)'' as a closed subscheme of
2956	  \(X\).
2957	\end{definition}

2959	\begin{definition}\leanok
2960	[Schematic-support immersion]
2961	  \label{def:schematic_support_immersion}
2962	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.schematicSupportι}
2963	  \uses{def:schematic_support, def:modules_annihilator}
2964	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\). The
2965	  \emph{schematic-support immersion} is the canonical closed immersion
2966	  \[
2967	    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F}) \;\hookrightarrow\; X
2968	  \]
2969	  of the schematic support (\cref{def:schematic_support}) into the ambient
2970	  scheme, namely the subscheme immersion of the annihilator ideal sheaf
2971	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}). It is a
2972	  quasi-compact preimmersion onto the support, and it is the morphism whose
2973	  composite with a base morphism \(f : X \to S\) defines proper support
2974	  (\cref{def:has_proper_support}). Proved directly in Lean.
2975	\end{definition}

2977	\begin{lemma}[Properness as a morphism property]
2978	  \label{lem:isProper_mathlib}
2979	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper}
2980	  \mathlibok
2981	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
2982	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Proper}).}
2983	  A morphism \(f : X \to S\) of schemes is \emph{proper} iff it is separated,
2984	  universally closed, and locally of finite type. Properness is stable under base
2985	  change: if \(f : X \to S\) is proper and \(g : T \to S\) is any morphism then
2986	  the base change \(X \times_S T \to T\) is proper. Consequently the base-change
2987	  clause that Nitsure invokes (``properness \ldots\ preserved by base-change'')
2988	  applies directly to the proper-support condition below.
2989	\end{lemma}

2991	\begin{definition}
2992	
2993	\leanok
2994	[Proper support over the base]
2995	  \label{def:has_proper_support}
2996	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.HasProperSupport}
2997	  \uses{def:schematic_support, def:schematic_support_immersion, lem:isProper_mathlib}
2998	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors $\Hilb_{X/S}$ and
2999	  % $\Quot_{E/X/S}$" (read from
3000	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L407-L409
3001	  % + L418-L419).
3002	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
3003	  % "(i) a coherent sheaf $\F$ on $X_T = X\times_ST$
3004	  %  such that the schematic support of $\F$ is proper over $T$
3005	  %  and $\F$ is flat over $T$, together with
3006	  %  [...]
3007	  %  Then as properness and flatness are preserved
3008	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,"
3009	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
3010	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\) and let
3011	  \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism. We say \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{has proper support
3012	  over \(S\) (along \(f\))} when the composite morphism
3013	  \[
3014	    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F}) \hookrightarrow X \xrightarrow{f} S
3015	  \]
3016	  is proper (\cref{lem:isProper_mathlib}). Since properness is stable under base
3017	  change (\cref{lem:isProper_mathlib}), this condition is preserved by pull-back
3018	  along any \(S'\to S\), which is exactly what the well-definedness of the Quot
3019	  functor's pullback action requires.
3020	\end{definition}

3022	\begin{definition}
3023	
3024	\leanok
3025	[Locally free of rank \(d\)]
3026	  \label{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
3027	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank}
3028	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Elementary Examples, Exercises (2)" and

3030	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L562-L564
3031	  % + L584-L585).
3032	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
3033	  % "$\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
3034	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
3035	  %  [...]
3036	  %  the set of all equivalence classes $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients
3037	  %  $q: E_T \to \F$ where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$,"
3038	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2) (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
3039	  Let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\) and let
3040	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). We say \(\mathcal{M}\) is \emph{locally free of rank
3041	  \(d\)} when there exists an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) together with
3042	  isomorphisms \(\mathcal{M}|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\oplus d}\) for

3048	\section{The Quot functor}
3049	\label{sec:quot_functor}
3050	
3051	\begin{definition}\leanok
3052	[Quot functor]
3053	  \label{def:quot_functor}
3054	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotFunctor}
3055	  \uses{def:hilbert_polynomial, def:has_proper_support}
3056	
3057	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) and
3058	  % \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\)" (read from
3059	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L394-L433
3060	  % + L481-L498).
3061	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
3062	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(X\to S\) be a finite type
3063	  %  scheme over it. Let \(E\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X\).
3064	  %  Let \(Sch_S\) denote the category of all locally noetherian schemes
3065	  %  over \(S\). For any \(T\to S\) in \(Sch_S\), a \textbf{family of quotients of \(E\)
3066	  %  parametrised by \(T\)} will mean
3067	  %  a pair \((\F,q)\) consisting of
3068	  %
3069	  %  (i) a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on \(X_T = X\times_ST\)
3070	  %  such that the schematic support of \(\F\) is proper over \(T\)
3071	  %  and \(\F\) is flat over \(T\), together with
3072	  %
3073	  %  (ii) a surjective \({\mathcal O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism of sheaves
3074	  %  \(q:E_T  \to \F\) where \(E_T\) is the pull-back of \(E\) under
3075	  %  the projection \(X_T \to X\).
3076	  %
3077	  %  Two such families \((\F,q)\) and \((\F,q)\) parametrised by \(T\) will
3078	  %  be regarded as equivalent if \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\)),
3079	  %  and \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) will denote
3080	  %  an equivalence class. Then as properness and flatness are preserved
3081	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,
3082	  %  the pull-back of \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) under an \(S\)-morphism \(T'\to T\) is
3083	  %  well-defined, which gives
3084	  %  a set-valued contravariant functor
3085	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S} : Sch_S \to Sets\) under which
3086	  %  \(\)T\mapsto  \{\mbox{ All } \langle \F, q \rangle \mbox{ parametrised by }T\}\(\)
3087	  %  [...]
3088	  %  This shows that the functor \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\) naturally decomposes
3089	  %  as a co-product
3090	  %  \(\)\Quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\(\)
3091	  %  where for any polynomial \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\), the functor
3092	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) associates
3093	  %  to any \(T\) the set of all equivalence classes of
3094	  %  families \(\langle \F,q \rangle\) such that at each \(t\in T\) the
3095	  %  Hilbert polynomial of the restriction \(\F_t\), calculated
3096	  %  using the pull-back of \(L\), is \({\Phi}\)."
3097	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
3098	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
3099	  \(L\) a line bundle on \(X\), \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\), and
3100	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). The \emph{Quot functor with Hilbert
3101	  polynomial \(\Phi\)}
3102	  \[
3103	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set}
3104	  \]
3105	  sends an \(S\)-scheme \(T \to S\) to the set of equivalence classes
3106	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) of pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) where
3107	  \begin{enumerate}
3108	    \item \(\mathcal{F}\) is a coherent sheaf on \(X_T = X \times_S T\) whose
3109	      schematic support is proper over \(T\) (\cref{def:has_proper_support}) and
3110	      which is flat over \(T\);
3111	    \item \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) is a surjective
3112	      \(\mathcal{O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism, where \(E_T\) is the pull-back
3113	      of \(E\) along \(X_T \to X\);
3114	    \item for every \(t \in T\) the Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}\)
3115	      with respect to \(L|_{X_t}\) (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}) equals
3116	      \(\Phi\).
3117	  \end{enumerate}
3118	  Two pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) and \((\mathcal{F}', q')\) are equivalent iff
3119	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). On morphisms \(f : T' \to T\) over \(S\), the functor
3120	  acts by pulling back the quotient along \(X_{T'} \to X_T\) (well-defined
3121	  since tensor product is right-exact and preserves both flatness and
3122	  properness). The Hilbert scheme is the special case
3123	  \(\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} := \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\).
3124	\end{definition}

3126	\section{The Grassmannian scheme}
3127	\label{sec:grassmannian_scheme}
3128	
3129	The Quot construction proceeds by embedding \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) as a
3130	locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian over \(S\). Since Mathlib carries
3131	no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} (only a finite-rank-quotient variety in
3132	linear algebra), we package the construction here.
3133	
3134	\begin{definition}\leanok
3135	[Grassmannian scheme]
3136	  \label{def:grassmannian_scheme}
3137	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian}
3138	  \uses{def:quot_functor, def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
3139	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Elementary Examples, Exercises~(2)" and
3140	  % "Construction of Grassmannian" (read from
3141	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L599
3142	  % + L773-L850).
3143	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
3144	  % "For any integers \(r\ge d\ge 1\), an explicit construction
3145	  %  the Grassmannian scheme
3146	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\), together with the tautological
3147	  %  quotient \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) where \
3148	  %  \(\U\) is a rank \(d\) locally free sheaf on \(\grass(r,d)\),
3149	  %  has been given at the end of this section. [...]
3150	  %  Show that \(\grass(r,d)\) together with the quotient
3151	  %  \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) represents the
3152	  %  contravariant functor
3153	  %  \(\)\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}\(\)
3154	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any \(T\) the
3155	  %  set of all equivalence classes
3156	  %  \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients \(q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F\)
3157	  %  where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\).
3158	  %  [...]
3159	  %  Using the above show that, more generally, if \(S\) is a scheme and
3160	  %  \(E\) is a locally free \({\mathcal O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\),
3161	  %  the functor \(\Grass(E,d)=\Quot_{E/S/S}^{d,{\mathcal O}_S}\)
3162	  %  on all \(S\)-schemes which
3163	  %  by definition associates to any \(T\)
3164	  %  the set of all equivalence classes \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients
3165	  %  \(q: E_T \to \F\) where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\),
3166	  %  is representable. The representing scheme
3167	  %  is denoted by \(\grass(E,d)\) and is called the rank \(d\)
3168	  %  relative Grassmannian of \(E\) over \(S\)."
3169	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2) and "Construction of
3170	  Grassmannian" (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
3171	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(V\) be a locally free
3172	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\). For an integer \(1 \le d \le r\), the
3173	  \emph{Grassmannian of rank-\(d\) quotients of \(V\)} is the functor
3174	  \[
3175	    \mathrm{Grass}(V, d) : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set},
3176	    \qquad
3177	    T \longmapsto \{ \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle :
3178	    q : V_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F},\; \mathcal{F}
3179	    \text{ locally free of rank } d \}
3180	  \]
3181	  with equivalence \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\); the rank-\(d\) local freeness of
3182	  \(\mathcal{F}\) is as in \cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}. Concretely,
3183	  \(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d) = \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_S}_{V/S/S}\): the Quot functor
3184	  (\cref{def:quot_functor}) in the special case \(X = S\), \(E = V\), constant
3185	  Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi = d\).
3186	\end{definition}

3188	\begin{lemma}[Representability predicate]
3189	  \label{lem:functor_is_representable_mathlib}
3190	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.IsRepresentable}
3191	  \mathlibok
3192	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
3193	  Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a category and \(F : \mathcal{C}^{op} \to \mathbf{Set}\)
3194	  a presheaf of sets. The predicate \(F.\mathrm{IsRepresentable}\) asserts that
3195	  \(F\) is representable, i.e.\ that there exists an object \(Y\) of
3196	  \(\mathcal{C}\) together with a natural bijection
3197	  \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathcal{C}}(-, Y) \cong F\) (the Yoneda bijection).
3198	\end{lemma}

  rather than distributing inline.

1196	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
1197	    \operatorname{restr}_{\iota_{R'}}\bigl(\operatorname{ext}_{\iota_A} M\bigr)
1198	    \;=\; (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M .
1199	  \]
1200	  Here the construction is parametrised by the leg-equality proofs
1201	  \((g', f', h_{\mathrm{fst}}, h_{\mathrm{snd}})\), which the isomorphism carries as data, with
1202	  \(g'\) and \(f'\) genuine free variables in the abstract statement. Instantiating at the literal
1203	  projections \(g' = \operatorname{pr}_1\), \(f' = \operatorname{pr}_2\) (with the cone-leg
1204	  identifications of Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_fst_snd_specMap_tensor}) recovers
